source.title: Identification of a novel metabolic engineering target for carotenoid production in Saccharomyces cerevisiae via ethanol-induced adaptive laboratory evolution

gist_memory.paper_problem_memory: The study aimed to improve carotenoid yield in engineered S. cerevisiae by discovering new metabolic engineering targets. Existing rational engineering had achieved high yields, but it was difficult to find new relevant targets. The hypothesis was that ethanol stress could be used as a selective pressure in adaptive laboratory evolution (ALE) to enrich mutants with enhanced carotenoid accumulation, based on the link between carotenoid production and membrane composition/ethanol tolerance. The goal was to apply ethanol-induced ALE to a carotenoid-producing strain, isolate hyper-producers, identify causal mutations via whole-genome resequencing, validate them by reverse engineering, and elucidate mechanisms through transcriptomics.

section_gists.heading: Abstract
section_gists.gist: A new ethanol-induced ALE was applied to a carotenoid producer BL03-D-4, yielding evolved strain M3 with a 5.1-fold increase in carotenoid yield. Whole-genome resequencing and reverse engineering identified loss-of-function mutation of PFK1 as the major cause. Transcriptome analysis revealed strengthening of gluconeogenesis and downregulation of cell wall-related genes. The study demonstrates using appropriate selective pressure to improve carotenoid yield via ALE and identifies PFK1 as a novel target.
process_signals: Ethanol-induced ALE was a novel approach to boost carotenoid accumulation.
M3 showed 5.1-fold increase in carotenoid yield.
PFK1 loss-of-function was causal.
Transcriptome provided mechanistic insights.

section_gists.heading: Introduction
section_gists.gist: Carotenoids are valuable but production is limited. Engineered S. cerevisiae can produce carotenoids, but discovering new targets is difficult. Random approaches like ALE can help. ALE with suitable stress can drive adaptation. Because carotenoid yield is linked to membrane engineering and ethanol tolerance, ethanol stress was hypothesized as a driving force for ALE to increase carotenoid accumulation. The study applies ethanol-induced ALE to strain BL03-D-4, identifies causal mutations, and explores mechanisms.
process_signals: Problem: difficulty in discovering new targets for carotenoid production in yeast.
Hypothesis: ethanol stress can be used as selective pressure because carotenoid accumulation may correlate with ethanol tolerance via membrane changes.
Previous ALE with hydrogen peroxide improved astaxanthin yield.
ALE is advantageous over random mutagenesis due to low mutation probability and ease of identifying causal mutations.

section_gists.heading: Material and methods
section_gists.gist: This section is a heading only, no content provided.

section_gists.heading: Adaptive laboratory evolution experiments
section_gists.gist: ALE was performed with parental strain BL03-D-4 in YPD media with different ethanol concentrations. Sequential passages and batch cultures were used. After cultivation, cells were plated for single colony isolation based on cell color, and colonies were screened further.
process_signals: ALE design: sequential passages with varying ethanol concentrations.
Screening based on cell color (darkened colonies).
methods: Sequential batch cultures in YPD with ethanol
Single colony isolation based on color

section_gists.heading: Results
section_gists.gist: This section is a heading only, no content provided.

section_gists.heading: Adaptive laboratory evolution of BL03-D-4 under ethanol stress
section_gists.gist: Ethanol-induced ALE was conducted with gradually increasing ethanol concentrations. Evolved strain M3 was isolated after three subcultures at 10% ethanol based on darkened color. A second round of evolution did not yield a better strain. M3 showed 5.1-fold increase in carotenoid yield in YPD and 2.4-fold in YPM. M3 had higher specific growth rate under 2-8% ethanol but no growth difference without ethanol. Ethanol supplementation repressed carotenoid yield in M3. Improvement was not due to biomass increase but likely metabolic changes.
process_signals: ALE terminated when growth plateaued; cells plated without ethanol to screen darkened colonies.
M3 obtained after three subcultures at 10% ethanol.
Second round of evolution failed to produce better strain.
Carotenoid yield increased 5.1-fold in YPD, 2.4-fold in YPM.
M3 growth rate improved under ethanol stress but not without ethanol.
Ethanol repressed carotenoid yield in M3.
Biomass not significantly different, so yield increase not due to growth.
methods: Shake-flask fermentation in YPD and YPM
Growth rate measurement under ethanol stress
failures_or_limits: Second round of evolution did not yield a better strain.
Ethanol supplementation repressed carotenoid yield in M3.

section_gists.heading: Whole-genome resequencing of the evolved strain M3
section_gists.gist: Whole-genome resequencing of BL03-D-4 and M3 identified 14 SNPs and 100 INDELs in M3. A loss-of-function mutation in PFK1 (stop codon) was found. PFK1 is involved in glycolysis/gluconeogenesis and NADPH generation. It was speculated that this mutation is related to improved carotenoid yield.
process_signals: Resequencing revealed PFK1 loss-of-function mutation.
PFK1 mutation hypothesized to affect carotenoid yield via metabolic rewiring.

section_gists.heading: Determining causal mutation
section_gists.gist: Reverse engineering: PFK1 deletion in BL03-D-4 (strain BE1) increased carotenoid yield to levels similar to M3, confirming PFK1 mutation as causal. BE1 had similar morphology to M3. PFK2 deletion (BE2) severely suppressed growth and did not increase carotenoids, but BE2 showed similar morphology after long cultivation. Microscopy showed M3, BE1, BE2 had larger cell size than BL03-D-4.
process_signals: PFK1 deletion recapitulated carotenoid phenotype, confirming causality.
PFK2 deletion did not increase carotenoids and impaired growth, suggesting PFK2 is the key isoenzyme.
Morphology changes: larger cells in evolved and knockout strains.
failures_or_limits: PFK2 deletion severely suppressed growth and did not increase carotenoids.

section_gists.heading: Transcriptome analysis of evolved strain M3
section_gists.gist: Transcriptome analysis of BL03-D-4 and M3 (cultured in YPM without ethanol, 24h) identified 37 up-regulated and 162 down-regulated genes (fold change >2). GO enrichment showed down-regulation of cell wall-related genes. KEGG enrichment indicated changes in methane metabolism, pentose phosphate pathway, glycolysis/gluconeogenesis, etc. The oxidative PPP genes were down-regulated, which was surprising given the need for NADPH. This suggests a novel mechanism for carotenoid yield improvement.
process_signals: Transcriptome revealed down-regulation of cell wall organization genes.
Oxidative PPP genes were down-regulated, contrary to expectation.
Suggests PFK1 mutation leads to a new mechanism for carotenoid increase.
failures_or_limits: Unexpected down-regulation of oxidative PPP genes.

section_gists.heading: Discussion
section_gists.gist: Ethanol stress involves many genes. ALE has been used for ethanol tolerance. Cell wall remodeling is important under ethanol stress. PFK1 mutation likely redirects carbon flux. In E. coli, pfkA deletion increases G6P and PPP flux, but here PPP was down-regulated. This suggests a different mechanism in yeast, possibly involving cell wall changes that affect membrane and carotenoid storage. The study provides a new strategy for improving carotenoid yield.
process_signals: PFK1 mutation effect differs from E. coli: PPP down-regulated instead of up-regulated.
Hypothesis: cell wall remodeling may affect membrane and carotenoid storage capacity.
Novel mechanism proposed.
failures_or_limits: Mechanism linking PFK1 mutation to carotenoid increase is not fully understood.

section_gists.heading: Conclusions
section_gists.gist: Ethanol-induced ALE successfully improved carotenoid yield 5.1-fold. PFK1 loss-of-function was identified as causal. Transcriptomics revealed gluconeogenesis strengthening and cell wall gene down-regulation. The study demonstrates ALE with appropriate pressure can improve yield and identifies PFK1 as a novel target.
process_signals: PFK1 is a novel metabolic engineering target.
ALE with ethanol stress is effective.

section_gists.heading: Availability of data and materials
section_gists.gist: Sequencing data deposited in GSA (CRA003704) and NCBI GEO (GSE164470). Strain M3 deposited at GDMCC (No. 61336).

global_hypotheses_about_storyline: The research evolved from the problem of finding new targets for carotenoid production to the hypothesis that ethanol stress could select for mutants with improved carotenoid accumulation.
ALE was designed with gradually increasing ethanol, and a hyper-producer M3 was isolated based on colony color.
Whole-genome resequencing identified PFK1 mutation as a candidate causal mutation.
Reverse engineering confirmed PFK1 loss-of-function increases carotenoid yield.
Transcriptomics revealed unexpected down-regulation of PPP and cell wall genes, suggesting a novel mechanism possibly involving cell wall remodeling and membrane changes.
The study concludes that ethanol-induced ALE is effective and PFK1 is a new target.
uncertainties_to_resolve: The exact mechanism by which PFK1 loss-of-function leads to increased carotenoid yield is not fully elucidated.
Why oxidative PPP was down-regulated despite increased carotenoid production is unclear.
The role of cell wall remodeling in carotenoid accumulation needs further investigation.
Potential trade-offs of PFK1 mutation (e.g., growth under certain conditions) were not fully explored.
Whether the approach is generalizable to other strains or products is unknown.

events.summary: Difficulty in discovering new targets relevant to carotenoid accumulation in engineered S. cerevisiae.
Ethanol stress could be used as a driving force for ALE to increase carotenoid accumulation, based on the link between carotenoid yield, membrane engineering, and ethanol tolerance.
Ethanol-induced ALE was designed with gradually increasing ethanol concentrations (2-12%) and sequential batch cultures, screening for darkened colonies.
A second round of evolution on M3 did not yield a better strain.
Shake-flask fermentation showed M3 had 5.1-fold increase in carotenoid yield in YPD and 2.4-fold in YPM compared to BL03-D-4.
Ethanol supplementation repressed carotenoid yield in M3, and biomass was not significantly different, indicating yield increase was not due to growth.
Whole-genome resequencing of BL03-D-4 and M3 was performed to identify genetic basis of improved yield.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk covers problem statement, hypothesis, ALE design, isolation of M3, failure of second round, yield quantification, growth analysis, and initiation of resequencing.

events.summary: Ethanol supplementation repressed carotenoid yield in M3, especially at high concentrations, and biomass was not significantly different, indicating yield improvement was not due to growth.
Whole-genome resequencing of M3 identified 14 SNPs and 100 INDELs, including a loss-of-function mutation in PFK1.
The loss-of-function mutation of PFK1 was speculated to be related to the promoted carotenoid yield.
Reverse engineering by deleting PFK1 in BL03-D-4 (strain BE1) increased carotenoid yield to levels similar to M3, confirming PFK1 mutation as causal.
Microscopy showed evolved and knockout strains had larger cell size than parental strain, possibly linked to carotenoid accumulation.
Transcriptome analysis of BL03-D-4 and M3 identified 37 up-regulated and 162 down-regulated genes, with GO enrichment showing down-regulation of cell wall-related genes.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk covers key results: ethanol repression analysis, whole-genome resequencing, reverse engineering validation, PFK2 deletion failure, morphology observation, and transcriptome analysis.

events.summary: Transcriptome analysis identified 37 up-regulated and 162 down-regulated genes in M3 compared to BL03-D-4.
KEGG enrichment showed differentially expressed genes primarily enriched in glycolysis/gluconeogenesis pathway, mostly down-regulated.
GO enrichment analysis showed differentially expressed genes mainly involved in cell wall organization, almost all down-regulated.
Oxidative pentose phosphate pathway genes were significantly down-regulated in M3, contrary to expectation for increased carotenoid production.
Hypothesis that PFK1 mutation leads to a new mechanism for improving carotenoid yield, possibly involving cell wall remodeling.
Unexpected down-regulation of oxidative PPP genes contradicts the expected need for NADPH in carotenoid biosynthesis.
M3 generally showed lower expression levels in PPP and cell wall-related genes than BL03-D-4.
Hypothesis that cell wall remodeling under ethanol stress may affect membrane and carotenoid storage capacity.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk contains transcriptome analysis results, enrichment analyses, and mechanistic hypotheses linking PFK1 mutation to carotenoid yield improvement via cell wall remodeling.

This chunk contains only supplementary material descriptions, acknowledgements, author contributions, funding, data availability, declarations, references, and publisher information. No research evolution events are described.

event_graph.paper_main_problem: Difficulty in discovering new metabolic engineering targets for carotenoid accumulation in S. cerevisiae.

canonical_events.summary: Difficulty in discovering new targets relevant to carotenoid accumulation in engineered S. cerevisiae.
Ethanol stress could be used as a driving force for ALE to increase carotenoid accumulation, based on the link between carotenoid yield, membrane engineering, and ethanol tolerance.
Ethanol-induced ALE was designed with gradually increasing ethanol concentrations (2-12%) and sequential batch cultures, screening for darkened colonies.
Evolved strain M3 was isolated after three subcultures at 10% ethanol based on darkened colony color.
A second round of evolution on M3 did not yield a better strain.
Shake-flask fermentation showed M3 had 5.1-fold increase in carotenoid yield in YPD and 2.4-fold in YPM compared to BL03-D-4.
Ethanol supplementation repressed carotenoid yield in M3, and biomass was not significantly different, indicating yield increase was not due to growth.
Whole-genome resequencing of BL03-D-4 and M3 identified 14 SNPs and 100 INDELs, including a loss-of-function mutation in PFK1.
The loss-of-function mutation of PFK1 was speculated to be related to the promoted carotenoid yield.
Reverse engineering by deleting PFK1 in BL03-D-4 (strain BE1) increased carotenoid yield to levels similar to M3, confirming PFK1 mutation as causal.
Microscopy showed evolved and knockout strains had larger cell size than parental strain, possibly linked to carotenoid accumulation.
Transcriptome analysis of BL03-D-4 and M3 identified 37 up-regulated and 162 down-regulated genes, with GO enrichment showing down-regulation of cell wall-related genes.
KEGG enrichment showed differentially expressed genes primarily enriched in glycolysis/gluconeogenesis pathway, mostly down-regulated.
Unexpected down-regulation of oxidative PPP genes contradicts the expected need for NADPH in carotenoid biosynthesis.
Hypothesis that PFK1 mutation leads to a new mechanism for improving carotenoid yield, possibly involving cell wall remodeling affecting membrane and carotenoid storage capacity.

edges.reason: The problem of finding new targets motivates the hypothesis that ethanol stress could be used as a selective pressure.
The hypothesis leads to the design of ethanol-induced ALE.
The ALE design results in the isolation of evolved strain M3.
After isolating M3, a second round of evolution was attempted but failed.
M3 is characterized for carotenoid yield in shake-flask fermentation.
Further analysis of M3 reveals ethanol repression and growth-independent yield increase.
The improved yield prompts whole-genome resequencing to identify causal mutations.
Identification of PFK1 mutation leads to the hypothesis that it is related to carotenoid yield.
The hypothesis is tested by reverse engineering PFK1 deletion.
Morphological analysis of knockout strains provides additional phenotypic evidence.
Validation of PFK1 as causal leads to transcriptome analysis to understand mechanism.
KEGG enrichment analysis of transcriptome data reveals pathway-level changes.
Transcriptome data unexpectedly shows down-regulation of oxidative PPP, contradicting expected NADPH demand.
The unexpected result leads to a new hypothesis about cell wall remodeling and carotenoid storage.

must_cover_points: Problem of finding new targets for carotenoid production
Hypothesis that ethanol stress can drive ALE for increased carotenoid accumulation
Design of ethanol-induced ALE with gradually increasing ethanol concentrations
Isolation of evolved strain M3 based on darkened colony color
5.1-fold increase in carotenoid yield in YPD
Whole-genome resequencing identifying PFK1 loss-of-function mutation
Reverse engineering confirming PFK1 mutation as causal
Transcriptome analysis revealing down-regulation of cell wall-related genes
Unexpected down-regulation of oxidative PPP genes
Hypothesis of new mechanism involving cell wall remodeling and carotenoid storage
